title: When the Bank of England Admitted the Textbooks Were Wrong

source.original: Where Does Money Come From? — Josh Ryan-Collins, Tony Greenham, Richard Werner, Andrew Jackson

body_html:
In April 2014, the Bank of England published a quarterly bulletin that quietly detonated a century of economic orthodoxy. The article, titled "Money Creation in the Modern Economy," stated plainly that the standard textbook account of how banks work is wrong. Commercial banks do not simply take deposits and lend them out. They create money in the act of lending. The money multiplier model taught in universities across the world does not accurately describe how the monetary system functions. This was not a radical journal. This was the central bank of the United Kingdom, in its official publication.

The bulletin was remarkable not for revealing something previously unknown to economists who study banking closely — researchers like Richard Werner (co-author of Where Does Money Come From?) had been making this argument for decades — but for the institutional admission. Central banks had generally allowed the intermediary theory to persist in public discourse and in university curricula. The 2014 bulletin broke that implicit arrangement. The BoE said, in plain language, that when banks make loans they create deposits out of thin air, that central banks do not control the money supply by controlling reserves, and that the textbook story is a pedagogical simplification that misleads more than it clarifies.

Why does this matter as a historical event rather than just as a technical clarification? Because institutional silence around monetary mechanics had served specific interests. The intermediary theory — banks as neutral conduits between savers and borrowers — legitimizes the banking sector's social role and obscures its money-creation privilege. If the public understood that private banks manufacture the money supply, questions would naturally arise: in whose interest is this creation directed? Who authorized this? What is the public getting in return for this subsidy? These are uncomfortable questions for an industry that profits enormously from a power delegated to it without explicit democratic deliberation.

The admission also validated a body of heterodox economic work that had been marginalized for decades. Post-Keynesian economists, Modern Monetary Theory proponents, and institutional economists had consistently argued that mainstream models treated money as neutral and banks as irrelevant to macroeconomics in ways that made their models useless for understanding financial crises. The 2008 financial crisis was, in retrospect, a definitive empirical test of these competing frameworks. Standard DSGE models — used by most central banks — did not include a meaningful banking sector and therefore could not model a banking crisis. The crisis happened anyway. This was an intellectual catastrophe that the BoE bulletin, in part, acknowledged.

The implications for economic education are still being worked out. CORE Economics, a curriculum reform initiative backed by major universities, incorporates the endogenous money view. But most undergraduate economics courses still teach the money multiplier as if the Bank of England had never published that bulletin. The gap between the frontier of central bank research and what students learn is wide enough to matter for policy: regulators, politicians, and journalists who don't understand how money is created will inevitably design interventions based on false premises.

The deeper lesson is about institutional knowledge management. The BoE's admission happened because the 2008 crisis made the old framework untenable. Crises are often when institutions finally say what they privately knew. This pattern — where the official story and the operational reality diverge, and the divergence only becomes public during systemic stress — is not unique to central banking. It is a feature of complex systems with concentrated information and distributed consequences. Understanding it is a prerequisite for building better institutions, whether those institutions are central banks, protocols, or anything in between.
